**Caddo County** is a [county](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)") located in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma"). As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the population was 26,945.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Its [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") is [Anadarko](/wiki/Anadarko,_Oklahoma "Anadarko, Oklahoma").[[2]](#cite_note-GR6-2) Created in 1901 as part of [Oklahoma Territory](/wiki/Oklahoma_Territory "Oklahoma Territory"), the county is named for the [Caddo](/wiki/Caddo "Caddo") tribe who were settled here on a reservation in the 1870s. Caddo County is immediately west of the seven-county Greater [Oklahoma City](/wiki/Oklahoma_City "Oklahoma City") metro area, and although is not officially in the metro area, it has many economic ties in this region.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Caddo_County,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Caddo County was organized on August 6, 1901, when the Federal Government allotted the [Kiowa](/wiki/Kiowa "Kiowa"), [Comanche](/wiki/Comanche "Comanche"), and [Arapaho](/wiki/Arapaho "Arapaho") reservations and sold the surplus land to white settlers.[[3]](#cite_note-caddo_county_history-3) The reservation land was part of Oklahoma Territory until Oklahoma became a state on November 16, 1907. Part of its land was taken at statehood to form neighboring [Grady County](/wiki/Grady_County,_Oklahoma "Grady County, Oklahoma"). Some additional land was taken in 1911 and also awarded to Grady County.[[3]](#cite_note-caddo_county_history-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Redstone_baptist_church.jpg/250px-Redstone_baptist_church.jpg)](/wiki/File:Redstone_baptist_church.jpg)

Redstone Baptist Church, north of the Apache Wye, Caddo County, [Kiowa](/wiki/Kiowa "Kiowa") mission founded in the 19th century.

Agriculture has been the mainstay of the local economy since its founding. The main crops were cotton, corn, wheat, alfalfa, [broom corn](/wiki/Broom_corn "Broom corn"), and [kaffir corn](/wiki/Kaffir_corn "Kaffir corn"). Poultry and livestock production have also been important. By 1960, Caddo County ranked first in Oklahoma for producing of peanuts, hogs and poultry.[[3]](#cite_note-caddo_county_history-3)

The first oil field (Cement Field) in the county was discovered in 1911, and oil production has remained important to the county economy since then. Smaller-scale booms in oil production occurred in the 1960s, 1970s and 1980s.[[3]](#cite_note-caddo_county_history-3)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Caddo_County,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

According to the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the county has a total area of 1,290 square miles (3,300 km2), of which 1,278 square miles (3,310 km2) is land and 12 square miles (31 km2) (0.9%) is water.[[4]](#cite_note-GR1-4) The county mostly lies in the Gypsum Hills and the [Red Bed](/wiki/Red_Beds_of_Texas_and_Oklahoma "Red Beds of Texas and Oklahoma") plains physiographic areas. The extreme southwestern corner is in the [Wichita Mountains](/wiki/Wichita_Mountains "Wichita Mountains"). The county is drained by the [Washita River](/wiki/Washita_River "Washita River"), Pond Creek and [Sugar Creek](/wiki/Sugar_Creek_(Caddo_County,_Oklahoma) "Sugar Creek (Caddo County, Oklahoma)"). Major reservoirs are [Chickasha Lake](/wiki/Chickasha_Lake "Chickasha Lake"), [Ellsworth Lake](/wiki/Lake_Ellsworth_(Oklahoma) "Lake Ellsworth (Oklahoma)"), and [Fort Cobb Lake](/wiki/Fort_Cobb_Lake "Fort Cobb Lake"),[[3]](#cite_note-caddo_county_history-3) [Red Rock Canyon State Park](/wiki/Red_Rock_Canyon_State_Park_(Oklahoma) "Red Rock Canyon State Park (Oklahoma)") near [Hinton](/wiki/Hinton,_Oklahoma "Hinton, Oklahoma") is notable for having the only remaining stand of native [Caddo maple](/wiki/Acer_saccharum "Acer saccharum") trees.

### 2020 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Caddo_County,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2020 census")]

As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the county had a population of 26,945. Of the residents, 21.8% were under the age of 18 and 17.5% were 65 years of age or older; the median age was 40.1 years. For every 100 females there were 125.2 males, and for every 100 females age 18 and over there were 130.9 males.[[11]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Census2020PL-12)

The racial makeup of the county was 62.1% White, 2.6% [Black or African American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), 21.4% [American Indian and Alaska Native](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), 0.3% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans"), 3.8% from some other race, and 9.8% from [two or more races](/wiki/Multiracial_Americans "Multiracial Americans"). [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") residents of any race comprised 16.1% of the population.[[12]](#cite_note-Census2020PL-12)

There were 9,284 households in the county, of which 31.5% had children under the age of 18 living with them and 28.2% had a female householder with no spouse or partner present. About 27.8% of all households were made up of individuals and 14.3% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older.[[11]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-11)

There were 11,544 housing units, of which 19.6% were vacant. Among occupied housing units, 72.4% were owner-occupied and 27.6% were renter-occupied. The homeowner vacancy rate was 2.6% and the rental vacancy rate was 11.0%.[[11]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-11)

### 2000 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Caddo_County,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2000 census")]

As of the [census](/wiki/Census "Census")[[13]](#cite_note-GR2-13) of 2000, there were 30,150 people, 10,957 households, and 7,965 families residing in the county. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 9/km2 (23/mi2). There were 13,096 housing units at an average density of 4/km2 (10/mi2). The racial makeup of the county was 65.55% [White](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 2.92% [Black](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)") or [African American](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 24.28% [Native American](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 0.17% [Asian](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 0.02% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 2.70% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 4.36% from two or more races. 6.28% of the population were [Hispanic](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)") of any race. 93.8% spoke [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), 4.5% [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") and 1.2% [Kiowa](/wiki/Kiowa_language "Kiowa language") as their first language.

There were 10,957 households, out of which 33.30% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 55.20% were [married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 13.00% had a female householder with no husband present, and 27.30% were non-families. 24.80% of all households were made up of individuals, and 12.50% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.62 and the average family size was 3.13.

In the county, the population was spread out, with 28.50% under the age of 18, 8.50% from 18 to 24, 26.00% from 25 to 44, 22.10% from 45 to 64, and 14.90% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 36 years. For every 100 females there were 98.60 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 96.00 males.

The median income for a household in the county was $27,347, and the median income for a family was $32,118. Males had a median income of $26,373 versus $18,658 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the county was $13,298. About 16.70% of families and 21.70% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 28.00% of those under age 18 and 15.90% of those age 65 or over.

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Caddo_County,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Politics")]

During the 20th century, Caddo County was a bellwether county: between 1908 and 2004, the county voted for the winner in every election but 1908, 1956, 1960 and 1988. However, while Democrats had a large plurality of registered voters through the 2010s, the county has recently been swept up in the growing Republican trend throughout Oklahoma. John McCain (2008), Mitt Romney (2012) and Donald Trump (2016) each received at least 64% of the county's vote.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Caddo_County,_Oklahoma&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Economy")]

Caddo County is home to cattle ranching and significant wheat and peanut farm operations—with a few of the producers practicing environmentally friendly no-till or reduced tillage farming methods.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

There is also one winery and vineyard in the county (Woods and Waters Winery and Vineyard).
